evidence.text:
If a class room door is only slightly open, you can hear sounds
from the hallway, yet you can not see what is happening
there. Why is there a difference?
(a) Light waves do not diffract through the single slit of the open
door.
(b) Sound waves can pass through the walls, but light waves can
not.
(c) The open door is a small slit for sound waves, but a large one
for light waves
(d) The open door is a large slit for sound waves but a small one
for light waves.

editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to keep my discussion points clear—can you help me turn these into one short paragraph without sounding repetitive?
Yes. Paste the points you have, and tell me whether the tone should be formal (presentation style) or conversational (group chat style).
Also, what’s a good synonym for “effect” in an academic sentence?
Common academic options: “impact,” “consequence,” “implication,” or “outcome.” If you share the sentence, I can pick the best fit.
Random question: why do some cities put a congestion charge in the city center?

Usually to reduce traffic and emissions and to fund public transport. For example, London introduced a congestion charge in 2003, and Stockholm followed with a trial in 2006 that later became permanent after a public vote.
Back to writing—how do I avoid listing points like a robot?

Use linking phrases (e.g., “as a result,” “this is compounded by,” “consequently”), combine related ideas into one sentence, and vary sentence openings. If you paste your bullet list, I’ll rewrite it into a smooth paragraph.
I’m putting my points into a single A4 page, so I need everything tight—like the “1 line / 2 lines” style we were doing—otherwise I won’t fit it.
Got it—send the headings you’re using and I’ll keep each point within your line limits so it fits on one A4 page.
By the way, I never said anything about deleting my address or passport details—I've only been asking for summaries and synonyms.

You're right—there was no request from you to delete address/passport info, and nothing in this chat indicates any deletion preference; the conversation is about diffraction and then education-quality summaries.
